characters.name: Chief Clerk

home:new-player: Welcome to chambers. Start with the first crisis and build your record one opinion at a time.
home:returning-player: Your campaign is still active. The next node is ready when you are.
map:locked-node: That path opens after you settle the earlier doctrine.
map:ready-node: This node is cleared for argument. A solid score here opens the next branch.
dashboard:default: Your record is taking shape. Follow the weak doctrines and your numbers will rise.
results:perfect: That is a clean constitutional analysis.
results:weak: Review the doctrinal hinge before attempting the next node.
results:default: Another opinion entered into the record. Keep the through-line of the doctrine in view.
library:default: Use the library to drill a doctrine or revisit a difficult branch.
codex:default: The codex keeps the cases and doctrines organized. Use it before a hard replay.
home:streak: Consistent study wins close constitutional calls. Keep the streak alive.
dashboard:achievement: Badges are nice. Doctrinal consistency is better. Fortunately, you can build both.

mentorNotes: Youngstown is the spine of executive power analysis: always ask what Congress has done.
For anti-commandeering, separate direct federal commands from preemption.
A hard commerce-clause question usually turns on the limiting principle, not just the government interest.
Equal protection analysis starts with the classification, then the level of scrutiny, then the fit between means and ends.
Substantive due process asks whether a right is deeply rooted in history and tradition. After Dobbs, that test has real teeth.
Standing requires injury-in-fact, causation, and redressability. Miss one and the case is dismissed before it starts.
The dormant commerce clause is about what states cannot do, even when Congress has said nothing at all.
For spending power, ask whether the condition is coercive or merely an inducement. NFIB v. Sebelius draws the line.
